topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. The subject line was innocuous enough—"Your recent piece on urban planning"—but the body of the message was a stark, undeniable fact: the byline was wrong. It was signed by a name that wasn't mine, a name belonging to a completely different journalist. I reread the text three times, my fingers trembling slightly as I scanned the meticulously formatted paragraphs. A strange, hollow feeling washed over me, as if the foundation of my recent work had suddenly dissolved. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small office, the cheap carpet muffling my hurried steps. Every surface seemed too bright, too sharp. I picked up my phone, intending to call the editor, but my voice caught in my throat, coming out as a reedy whisper.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the final paragraph. My breath hitched. It wasn't a typo; it was a complete substitution. The words I had poured my late nights into, the nuanced arguments I had painstakingly crafted, were suddenly tethered to someone else’s reputation. A flush crept up my neck, warm and undeniable, spreading across my cheeks. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under the sudden shift in my posture. My hands began to shake, and I had to press them flat against the desk to steady them. *Impossible*. The thought echoed in my mind, sharp and unwelcome. I closed my eyes, trying to force the swirling confusion down, but the silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the file, the reality hit with the force of a physical impact. My jaw went slack. I felt a sudden, dizzying disorientation, as if the floor had tilted beneath me. The names, the quotes, the very structure of my analysis—all belonging to another author. A low sound escaped my throat, a dry, involuntary noise. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafening in the quiet studio. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles turned white. Outside, the city moved on, indifferent to the sudden, internal earthquake I was experiencing. I could feel a desperate need to check the source, to confront the absurdity of the situation, but my limbs felt heavy, unresponsive.
